claimed_contribution: One of the first project-level studies of how cross-functional team characteristics and immediate team context relate to new product innovativeness.
Extends innovation research beyond macro firm-level antecedents by testing team-level and contextual drivers of innovativeness while accounting for organizational and market covariates.
Introduces a social identity-based perspective in this setting by emphasizing superordinate team identity as a way to reduce functional biases and improve innovative outcomes.
context: The study examines major new consumer product development projects run by cross-functional teams in consumer product manufacturing firms. Sampled industries include appliances, lawn care equipment, office supplies, toys, processed foods, health and beauty aids, and household products.
design_and_data: The authors conducted a mail survey of project managers from the marketing function who had recently coordinated cross-functional new product projects. Questionnaires were sent to 240 managers, and 141 usable responses were obtained (58.8% response rate). Respondents reported on their most recently completed qualifying project; average development time was 16.4 months, and projects were completed about 7.8 months before data collection. Hypotheses were tested with mean-centered moderated regression including team variables, contextual influences, interaction terms, and multiple covariates.
main_findings: New product innovativeness is positively related to superordinate identity, encouragement to take risk, customers' influence, and senior management monitoring of the project.
Beyond a moderate level, social cohesion is negatively related to innovativeness and it weakens the positive superordinate identity-innovativeness relationship.

Encouragement to take risk strengthens the positive effect of superordinate identity on innovativeness, while functional diversity shows no significant direct effect and no supported hypothesized nonlinear effects.
one_line_summary: Using survey data from 141 cross-functional consumer product development teams, the paper shows that superordinate identity and risk-supportive context improve product innovativeness, whereas high social cohesion can undermine it.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior innovation research that mainly emphasized firm-level antecedents and rates of innovation by focusing on project-level determinants of product innovativeness in cross-functional teams. It applies social identity logic to explain how functional biases inside teams influence innovative outcomes.
research_gap: Prior work largely examined organizational-level innovation (e.g., innovation rate or volume) and macro structural or cultural predictors, with little direct evidence on project-level determinants of product innovativeness. The paper also notes limited research on how cross-functional team dynamics and immediate contextual influences shape innovativeness.
research_question: How do characteristics of cross-functional product development teams and immediate contextual influences affect the innovativeness of new consumer products? The study also asks whether encouragement to take risk, social cohesion, and functional diversity moderate the effect of superordinate identity on innovativeness.
